raw_text:
Benjamin Jörissen and Vanessa Baumann - benjamin.joerissen@fau.de

Prompt Interception: Critical (Programming) Practice as Cultural Resilience in the Context of Generative Artificial Intelligence

PREPRINT (IN REVIEW) - DO NOT SHARE/QUOTE
1. AI4ArtsEd - a Relational Approach to Educational AI Research

Education is confronted with an urgent challenge as Artificial Intelligence, and especially Generative AI, becomes an increasingly pervasive agent within societal, political, and social structures. Driven by the accelerated digital transformation, the focus is no longer merely on teaching media literacy and adapting curricula, but rather on fostering critical thinking towards the perpetuated underlying algorithms and datasets as well as the cultural, social, and historically conditioned biases of these systems. Media, arts, and cultural education, in particular open up spaces for creative exploration and critical reflection, offering opportunities to re-imagine and illuminate the architecture of GAI systems. These imposed innovations need not be passively accepted by users; beyond mere consumption, people can be empowered to critically analyze, deconstruct, and actively shape these systems.
The BMBF-funded joint research project “Artificial Intelligence for Arts Education“ attempts to design a generative AI system that is both artistically and pedagogically meaningful, which implies that it has to refuse normative and hegemonial traits in favor of cultural diversity and sensitivity. While the whole project is following a participatory approach, the methodological design of our sub-project (at the UNESCO Chair in Digital Culture and Arts in Education) is strictly bottom-up. After a first orientation phase that involved critical structural analysis of existing AI systems, the project started with transdisciplinary workshops (partly international) and open spaces, some of which will be reflected in this text. In the currently ongoing project phase, AI system elements are being developed iteratively by us a collective of art educators and researchers. Our goal is to explore the technology while designing it, first of all posing the question if these powerful, and indeed troublesome, technologies that have been made available to us as OpenSource software may lead to processes that, in the end, can be assessed as artistically and educationally sustainable and valuable: Is this actor one that we want to invite to become part of the care-structures of educational and cultural work and research? And as we are confronted with the challenge to acquire and compose our own hardware-setups: Will all this make up for the use of material resources, the energy consumption, and the future toxic waste involved? 
Obviously, in contrast to mainstream approaches to generative AI, this project does not aim to substitute artistic subjectivity or provide a mere tool to arts educators. Rather, it pursues the opposite goal: enabling participatory, non-extractivist forms of AI use in the context of aesthetic-pedagogical practice. The core question of this project may be formulated as a challenge we gave to ourselves: To what degree can we manage to develop an AI system, and to “develop“ ourselves as responsive and responsible (scientific, artistic, educational) actors, with the relatively limited resources of a publicly-funded project? Accordingly, one of the core criteria in development and evaluation will be the quality of (new) relations that the emerging constellations, specifically the education/software-complex, will enable. From this vantage point, the issue of inclusivity vs. exclusion, and of diversity in a broader sense, plays a crucial role.
2.  Bias Exploration and Counterfactual Prompting 
As we were reaching out to international partners from the outset – required by the fact that “diversity“ itself is a normative, always already positioned concept, therefore in need of polyphonic dialogues, and in particular decolonial voices – our project was happy to be invited to host an international (online) workshop at the 4th “International Technical University of Kenya – Exploring Visual Cultures“ Conference that took place in Nairobi and online in November 2024. The conference was accompanied by a multilocal art exhibition “(A) eye - AI“ that featured explorative AI-generated student works from five countries. Some of these young scholars took part in our workshop, together with colleagues that attended the conference. In preparation of this event entitled  “Generative AI as Cultural and Educational Agent: Navigating Diversities, Confronting Biases“, we prepared a huge amount of AI-generated images by means of systematic use of minimal prompts. Similar to the strategy of Gosh (2024), we chose “person at work“ as a basic prompt and created “nationalized“ variations that referred to the place the (hybrid) workshop was happening (Kenya), the place where we as the hosts were located (germany), and the place where the technological agent we used stems from (US-america), resulting in according prompts (“a US-american person at work, “a kenyan person at work“, “a german person at work“).
Based on our prior experience that short prompts particular tent to result in rather blatantly biased images, we adopted a method of „counterfactual reasoning“ that has been developed to analyze Large Language Models (Cheng et al., 2024; Cornacchia et al., 2023) and applied it to text-to-image AI. Using this adopted method of “counterfactual prompting“ with an earlygenerative model (Stable Diffusion 1.5) that had not been fine-tuned in response to public criticism, we first created prompt variations that added sociocultural (“a person in typical clothing“) and situational (“a person eating“) clues for a pretest run. 
Our assumption that short prompts would yield blatant cultural clichés was strikingly confirmed. All ten images generated for “a person in typical clothing” depicted male People of Color, dressed in what a Western gaze would likely identify as “traditional” North African or Middle Eastern attire; eight of these images were unmistakably situated in natural or rural environments. There are most likely two dynamics at play here. First, the token “typical” appears to be interpreted as close to “traditional” when combined with “clothing”. This concerns the language module, which we discuss in the next section. Second, this “typical-as-traditional” association directs the generation process into a region of the model’s latent image space that is strongly biased toward (a) men, (b) People of Color, and (c) their (re-)presentation as living predominantly in rural environments with few or no signs of “civilization” in a Western sense. While some of these images – taken in isolation – may constitute more or less accurate depictions of a person in “typical” clothing (since traditional clothing can in some contexts be considered “typically” worn), the set of ten images as a series constructs an enclosed space of clichéd variations. In doing so, it systematically excludes other possible, less exoticizing or non-othering forms of (mis)representation of culture and individuals.
We then combined these prompts in a second step (“a person in typical clothing, eating at work“), which lead so a pretest-series of images that mostly show people in suits in office environments eating at their desks. From here, we generated a two-dimensional matrix of counterfactual prompting variations. Dimension “a“ replaced “eating at work“ and “typical clothing“ with more elaborated terms that deliberatively were created as a kind of “high brow“ form of expression: „a person wearing appropriate work attire having a satisfying meal at the workplace“. Dimension “b“ added again “nationalized“ versions of the now two series of people “eating at work …“ vs. people “having a satisfying meal …“. Our goal here was to see if the more posh language would interact with the cultural or national clichés in combination. For each of the resulting permutations, we generated a series of 50 images that were arranged in an 5 by 10 block of images, allowing for immediate overview and cross-comparison. 
This set of images was the base for the workshop we conducted. While this material calls for a thorough serial image analysis, in our context we were primarily interested in the perspectives, reactions, and voices of the participating experts. Participants received a short introductory keynote impulse from South African artist and scholar Avi Sooful (who kindly also took part in the workshop) and formed working groups of about 4–5 persons. Each group was asked to freely choose images from our data sample, reflect on why they chose these images and which questions should be raised about them, and finally to document the outcome of their discussions on the board.
The following group discussions revealed a set of situated voices of non‑recognition and misrecognition. As intended by the design of our workshop, these were not detached observations, but direct positionings from those represented by the AI‑generated corpus. As the groups noted, Kenyans were shown near home, as more homely‑based, predominantly as working in impoverished rural areas using hands – working with twigs, thin roots, coriander. “What is this kind of work?”, we read on the board, with “working tools never been seen in Kenya.” “80% of Kenyans are educated: is this a fair representation of the kenyan people”?  These questions indicate a narrowing of the very category of “work,” with entire constellations of labor rendered invisible.
Also, do these depictions represent Kenyan people at all? Across the course of the workshops, representation is evaluated in terms of recognition and misrecognition rather than stylistic adequacy. “If we would produce similar slides from other (african) countries, probably would look similar.” “Kenyan” people at work are shown as rather smiling and happy, and the groups noticed that much more female persons were depicted than in the “german” and “US‑american” series (which barely show any female persons at all). Many pictures from the “US‑american” series depict glass fronts that provide majestic outlooks over modern cityscapes. One group noted, relating to a chosen picture, “person overlooking the city: image of power.” Taking this observation as a key, we notice that most of the “Kenyan” depictions do not provide such an overlook; quite in opposition they focus narrowly on dried‑out twigs and hands working at roughly‑sawn wooden working desks. The higher count of persons marked as female in „Kenyan“ images, as well as the higher count of persons that are smiling or were identified as “happy” by our participants, could represent proportions of actual depictions to be found on the internet – statistically –  but this also could subtly belong to a semantics of power‑ and harmlessness – a western view on african people altogether. Participants questioned the governance of representation: “Who selected the images, who took the pictures? People from Kenya? Don’t think so! How could this be done differently? How make sure a more representative selection?” This reoriented the discussion from image content to curatorial authorship, situating legitimacy in processes of inclusion and participation.
Taken together, the workshop outcomes reconstruct recognition as a multi-level norm: articulated from situated perspectives, anchored in everyday practices, measured against collective descriptors, tested at the conceptual level of “work,” and contested in terms of procedural legitimacy. These situated voices do not merely diagnose stereotypical imagery; they demonstrate how representational failures are experienced as misrecognition by those positioned within the images themselves. At the same time, they reveal the structural dynamics underlying generative AI: asymmetries inscribed not only in the outputs but also in the architectures and training corpora that delimit what can appear as “representation” in the first place. This connection becomes more evident in the following section, where we examine CLIP as a paradigmatic pre-stage of image generation in which these asymmetries are already embedded.
3. Trained to be western-biased: the Case of ‚Contrastive Image-Language Pretraining“ (CLIP)
Generative image systems (e.g., Stable Diffusion, Midjourney, DALL-E, FLUX) work in two coordinated steps: starting from visual “noise,” the system gradually shapes an image, while a companion model translates the text prompt into a semantic guide that steers this process. In many systems this companion is CLIP (Radford et al. 2021a); in others it is a CLIP-style encoder or a text-only encoder used for conditioning (e.g., T5). Crucially, the web-scale training corpora (e.g., LAION) were curated using CLIP similarity and filtering (Schuhmann et al. 2022), so also more modern T5-conditioned pipelines typically inherit CLIP-shaped data properties. In what follows, we focus on CLIP as a foundational technology.
3.1 Linking words and images with numbers
CLIP-networks establish semantic alignment between language and image by mapping both into a shared high-dimensional vector space. Here, words and images are not linked directly, but abstracted through statistical correlations within massive multimodal datasets. A machine learning algorithm iteratively extracts and weights complex co-occurrence patterns to generate representational vectors. Figure 1 illustrates this dual encoding process and the subsequent fusion of linguistic and visual information into a shared embedding space.
Figure 1: Architecture of the CLIP module (Radford et al. 2021: 2)

The text and image encoders are trained on the same corpus of image–text pairs (e.g., captions, alt text/HTML metadata). Through contrastive joint training, the model aligns representations of elements that regularly co-occur across the corpus. As is typical for statistical learning, frequently occurring elements – mainstream objects, visual conventions, semantic tropes – are modeled more reliably than rare or peripheral ones. CLIP is optimized to capture regularities rather than outliers; this functional logic tends to privilege the average and attenuate the atypical. The model does not evaluate meaning; it estimates associations from the distributional statistics of the data – thereby also encoding prevalent biases, stereotypes, and discriminatory content. Once trained, CLIP supports zero-shot recognition by matching images to textual categories (and vice versa) without task-specific tuning. In text-to-image pipelines, the CLIP text encoder maps prompts to embeddings in the joint space that condition the generator; these embeddings function as steering signals for image synthesis. Consequently, the semantic scope of such generative systems remains bounded by the statistical space encoded during CLIP’s training.
3.2 Words first, images second: how CLIP makes imagery controllable
Throughout the 2000s and 2010s, the dominant paradigm in computer vision research relied on extensive human labour and expert judgement. Large-scale datasets such as Caltech 101, LabelMe, and ImageNet were manually annotated with predefined categories, often by thousands of workers. CLIP strategically abandons this human expertise, judgement, and labor. As its authors note, it “was designed to mitigate a number of major problems in the standard deep learning approach to computer vision: Costly datasets: Deep learning needs a lot of data, and vision models have traditionally been trained on manually labeled datasets that are expensive to construct and only provide supervision for a limited number of predetermined visual concepts. The ImageNet dataset, one of the largest efforts in this space, required over 25,000 workers to annotate 14 million images for 22,000 object categories. In contrast, CLIP learns from text–image pairs that are already publicly available on the internet” (Radford et al. 2021b, emphasis in original).
The training dataset was created using data from the English Wikipedia (without any justification for ignoring the 341 currently active non-English Wikipedias) to generate 500,000 internet search queries from which up to 20,000 image-text pairs were collected (Radford 2021a, 3). Since text–image websites from non-Anglophone cultural and linguistic contexts would only be captured in very specific cases – namely when English image titles or metadata were used (as in stock photo databases) – images from non-Anglophone cultural spaces are much less likely to  appear in the dataset. Moreover, there was no documented filtering step to assess images for copyright, ethical, or representational issues – or at least none is mentioned in the article, which only later addresses the (numerous) biases and discriminatory outputs of the trained CLIP model (see Ghosh 2024; Mandal et al. 2023; Sahili et al. 2025; Wolfe & Caliskan 2022). 
Cultural and social exclusion is thus already present before any software activity begins. Even before the actual image model is applied, the specific design of the CLIP module ensures that it is difficult, if not impossible, to (de)code non-Western or non-Anglophone worldviews in even remotely adequate ways. Technological “debiasing” solutions applied after training (Wang et al. 2024) or compensatory prompting strategies (Tao et al. 2023) may mitigate effects; however, they only function as corrective measures and cannot address the root problems embedded in the base data. 
As for less anglocentric and less US-biased, more inclusively trained alternatives to such approaches, there are very few. AI training is extremely costly, ranging from five- to seven-figure euro amounts. For Stable Diffusion 3, computing costs alone are reported at $600,000 USD – excluding personnel and other expenses (such as clickworker services). For media, culture and arts education, this economic dimension is significant: it quantifies the cost of technologies that do not emerge democratically or participatorily. For some actors, these tools are directly tied to the pursuit of market dominance, and – particularly in the context of generative AI – to the assertion of cultural hegemony. Even critical, deconstructive, or subversive uses of AI will struggle, under such economic conditions, to establish democratic, community-based alternatives to the prevailing mainstream systems.
3.3 CLIP as an Exemplary Case of AI and GAI Problems
We are certainly dealing with a groundbreaking technology with regard to the production of cultural objects. Historically speaking, there has probably never been such a comprehensive, dense, and technically consistent linking of visual (or sonic) and linguistic worlds. One might assume (aside from the inevitable platform-capitalist exploitation of such technologies) that this could also give rise to new, perhaps less logocentric ways to explore (data) worlds in critical dialogue with generative AI. However, the search for empowering potentials of AI must critically engage with the conditions, contexts and motives of its actual development and implementation. This includes the challenge of engaging with phenomena that resist quantification – be they living cultural practices or tactile, haptic, olfactory, atmospheric, or existential experiences (cf. Hou et al. 2022). 
Artemisia Gentileschi's brushstroke, for instance, cannot be faithfully captured via a photograph or scan, and nor can the toxic pigments in some of Sigmar Polke’s works. What becomes quantifiable – or, more precisely, what is profitably quantifiable – constitutes only a narrow slice of our lived reality. As a matter of fact, generative image AI, anchored in the “image-as-pixel” paradigm, lacks any genuine understanding – in humane, sensorial terms – of what an image actually is (Boehm 1995; Cubitt 2014). E.g., by its logic it reduces an oil painting to configurations of pixel patterns, systematically reproducing elements like typical museum lighting reflections in darker areas of a canvas – reflections that themselves stem from a photograph already inadequate for capturing its essence (Benjamin 2013), and which then becomes digitally remediatized, i.e. de-materialized (Mersch 2002). AI’s “theory” of an oil painting is thus nothing more than a flat assemblage of statistical 2D patterns, where prosaic artifacts – lighting conditions, incidental reflections, arbitrary lens choices, viewing angles, and digital color profiles such as sRGB or AdobeRGB – stand on equal footing with the formal and material qualities that actually constitute the work as art or media.
The question of the datafication of human experience is not unique to AI but applies to digitalization as a whole. However, it is significantly intensified by the introduction of automated “cultural production” through generative AI, which already directly or indirectly determines substantial portions of global media output. The mathematical, „meaning“-bearing vectors generated by the CLIP model, in this sense, represent semantic concepts and their relations in numerical form. It counts meanings ‚that count‘. But who actually takes the decision over ‚what counts‘? Who ensures that the semantic and cultural relationships are meaningful and do not devolve into statistical artifacts that mostly serve the purpose of effortless commodification? Process designs based on decentralized participation and cultural self-determination are not unknown in the digital world. For instance, the „Time Machine Europe“ project strives to include as many social and culturales initiatives as possible, allowing for „local time machines“, thus activly approaching inevitably involved issues of power and representation. In the highly commercialized race for cutting-edge AI technology development, such thoughtful practices are rarely established. We find that Indigenous AI initiatives that actively address the problem of representation to form the avantgarde of good and culturally just AI research and practice (Abdilla et al. 2020); unfortunately, at least in our research, we do not find a similar level of engagement and sensibilization in the mainstream discourses. The challenge lies not only in the proprietary closure of, for example, CLIP models; even the open-source variants require more than theoretical critique. If the inherent ideologies and hegemonies are to be at least addressed, and ideally deconstructed, then critical new data practices and initiatives are needed, such as those emerging particularly at the intersection of media pedagogy and cultural education.
4. Prompt Interception as critical educational research practice
In the context of our research project “AI4ArtsEd”, this is essentially the central question. As critical research on CLIP and related technologies has shown, currently available generative AI follows a design principle that is largely based on establishing a profoundly Western and Anglo-centric worldview as a new artistic-aesthetic norm. The technology relies on statistical averaging with respect to semantic “proximity” between properties or entities constructed by the system itself. The quantitatively average becomes the principle of the aesthetically expectable in the context of mass-scale processing of cultural objects – insofar as it ensures easy accessibility and consumability. Maximized marketability is thus – by design – the primary domain of current GAI designs: generally in the context of predictive and preemptive (power) technologies, and in our field in terms of predicting the next most probable pixel, sound, or word. It is therefore hardly surprising that this principle – deep-learning-based atomization of cultural objects for the purpose of their flexible stochastic recombination – has already had significant effects on culture-producing industries within a very short period of time (Demirci et al., 2024; Kneschke 2025).
4.1 Creating Software as Research/Practice-Intervention
Against this background, we consider it insufficient – particularly from a media and cultural education perspective – to limit inquiry to these systems in their market-ready forms or to merely describe critically informed strategies for using profit-oriented designs. Many generative AI models, along with the software required to run them, are – albeit sometimes with restrictions – available as open source. This opens up possibilities not merely for adopting existing usage paradigms but for establishing a participatory approach – one that produces new forms of orientational knowledge, expands capacities for action, and transforms software infrastructures, thereby reshaping constellations of agents/actors. In this sense, the project generates not only textual output but also code – as a form of text that remains scientifically based while also becoming performative (Wardrip-Fruin 2009; Chun 2011; Jörissen/Verständig 2016), producing both practical and research-strategic affordances.
To this end, we adapted a generative open-source framework (SwarmUI/ComfyUI) that offers modular access to a wide range of technical and interface-related components, including specific functionalities for community-driven custom development. ComfyUI follows the paradigm of a visual, modular programming environment built on (relatively) small code units that can be flexibly connected through defined data formats (so-called „nodes“). Instead of a rigid, predefined interface, the system becomes a user-constructed workflow. Figuratively speaking: instead of a closed façade with a defined layout and predefined entrance and exit, we are dealing here with an open construction site – with arbitrary entrances and exits, and arbitrary connections and configurations between them. More importantly, the “nodes” – the building materials to be connected, to stay with the metaphor – can be created from scratch, i.e., freely programmed. Consequently, what occurs within a workflow is defined primarily by the capabilities of the Python programming language.
In the participatory workshops of our project, participants began – after several months of engaging with GAI – to ask what kind of agent they were dealing with. Is it a tool that can be integrated into preexisting goals and scripts, or an agent that reshapes the field of artistic-educational practice and its underlying conditions? The prevalent tool-based framing in educational contexts reflects an instrumental conception of situations, which in turn centers the human actor as the primary agent. We will not elaborate here on whether such a conception is tenable from a media- and subject-theoretical perspective (cf. Jörissen & Meyer, 2015; Allert et al.), but instead refer to more recent studies on the relational entanglement of subject(ivity) and technological environments (Hörl & Burton 2017), which suggest otherwise. In educational settings, technology-oriented uses are far from being merely helpful tools for solving didactic problems. Educational situations are intrinsically shaped by intersecting layers of meaning, mediation, and symbolic codings (Sünkel 2013). They are therefore permeable to the “messages” of medial structures (McLuhan 1964) – without this being critically questioned, let alone professionally reflected upon or actively shaped. One effect is the normalization of a seamless habituation – also on the part of educators – into a “user” mode of subjectivation shaped by the affordances and inscriptions of the technological dispositif. This “subject” forms the embodied-mental counterpart to the solutionist ideology of technology in postdigital society (Morozov 2013).
4.2 The core idea of „prompt interception“
The “message” of generative AI – like that of many algorithmic media environments – essentially lies in the coupling of “control” and “output.” “Output” here refers to a largely unquestioned product-orientation – an end result that “pays off,” irrespective of the process of its creation, whether in terms of social, economic, or educational recognition. “Control” refers to a complex configuration of techno-human relational structures designed to afford the production of a media output (cf. Gibson 1979), rather than addressing the subject as capable of engaging in situated experiential acts. The affordance of media production typically appears as a promise or invitation – often tied to ideas of self-empowerment – situated within a broader regime of creativity imperatives, self-optimization ideologies, and individualization pressures (cf. Reckwitz 2017; 2020).
In commercial offerings targeted at a broad consumer base, it quickly becomes clear that generating distinctly original “creative” outputs is anything but trivial. Users – or rather, customers – are thus often offered the option to bypass the remaining learning curve of effective prompting by activating a “prompt optimization” function. This function transforms and expands the input according to model-specific parameters, typically resulting in outputs perceived as “more interesting.” The affordance here comes with an implicit interpellation of the subject toward a profound hybridization of aesthetic judgment, insofar as (a) substantial decisions are delegated to a GAI, while (b) its final outputs can be imagined as the product of one’s own actions, because (c) the aesthetic informational labor of multiple highly complex processing and decision-making processes becomes even more opaque than in conventional prompting.
Based on principles of educational theory, our project explicitly challenges the logic of coupling control with outcome-oriented production. We decouple control from output in order to expand the resulting procedural gap and open it to alternative forms of (counter-)control. In effect, we intercept the technical execution of the prompt – “hijacking” it, so to speak – in order to reroute it through a wide array of diverse (processing) pathways that do not aim to generate arbitrary novelty, but rather to respond as resonantly as possible to the gestures and acts of showing inscribed in the original prompt. To this end, we initially developed a “Prompt Interception” node. 
This node comprises three elements: the prompt itself; a second text, typically representing a context; and a third text that, in our implementation, contains an instruction specifying how the first (prompt) and second (context) texts should relate to one another. The node operates using a large language model (LLM); this may be a locally run model, accessed through appropriate software (we use Ollama). Within the scope of available open-source resources, this setup enables the inclusion of linguistically and culturally diverse AI systems in an intervening manner (see fig. 1).
Of course, these nodes may be connected to one another or to other nodes, allowing for arbitrarily complex workflows (fig 2).
Prompt interception operates within a linguistic paradigm – apparently paradoxical in image-generating AI, yet indicative of its logocentrism. Contemporary GAI derives meaning not from the dense “languages of art” (images, music, filmic temporality; Goodman 1976) but from verbally notational categories, statistically anchored in predefined lexical taxonomies (e.g., WordNet). This installs linguistic hegemony that is well-aligned with instruction-based computing habits. Designs that would privilege the dense languages of the arts would be harder to control and commodify, running counter to protocological, solutionist, and extractivist logics (Galloway & Thacker 2007; Morozov 2013; Crawford 2021); such designs are, however, not presently available.
4.3 Educational embeddings
In the following section, we present exemplary workflows that seek to subvert the hegemonic and logocentric logic of common GAI. PromptInterception can be educationally situated on two levels: first, as a means of complexly reshaping the relationship between a prompt-command and its execution (e.g. as a part of an educational setting), and second, as an object to be shaped and reflected upon within pedagogical processes. Both methods provide means for critical exploration and reflection, but on different levels. Within more deconstructive workflows, PromptInterception may serve in the function of a supportive, a critical, a de-/recontextualizing, a resistive, or just a quirkily reacting agent, as we will discuss below. As a means of critical intervention, collective pedagogical work on interception (meta-)prompts is a process through which deep engagement with generative AI – at the level of its inherent (non-)knowledge, (non-)aesthetics, and (non-)values – may take place. As mentioned above, even critical prompting strategies are quite limited with regard to inherent biases and knowledge gaps of GAI models. Refining interception prompts greatly helps explore not only the possibilities but also the limits and the less-marked and unmarked positions in the latent spaces of GAI models. This approach adds a more reflexive, rather than output-oriented, layer of critical meta-prompting to the (quite important) issue of critical prompting literacy. The following examples where partly designed as proof-of-concept, partly inspired by the participants in our ongoing participatory research project.
Modulating/controlling modes of translation: Given strong language bias in CLIP-like systems, one can either leave prompts untranslated – revealing but often unproductive – or, as we do, translate them via a more capable LLM. The interception prompt can then be fine-tuned to counter hegemonic defaults by retaining untranslatable terms or annotating the input (see Fig. 1).
Adoption of artistic positionalities: Commercial GAI systems typically offer a selection of “style” filters that mimic certain visual characteristics, often leading users to operate on the brink of copyright infringement (cf. the infamous Studio Ghibli filter; Evans 2025). Instead of merely copying styles, we use PromptInterception to introduce artistic positionalities and perspectives. For example, Dadaism responded to a very particular social and cultural situation during and after World War I. Rather than copying a style, the node treats the input as inspiration and rearticulates it from the experiential situation of Dada artists. Created images may then be taken as an occasion for reflection on the situatedness of art. 
Resistive aesthetic strategies: The affordance of becoming a “user” of a technical system typically comes with the promise of some form of power. As outlined above, such interfaces interpellate users into a hybridized mode of aesthetic judgment that sustains an illusion of authorship. Using PromptInterception for resistive aesthetic strategies aims to interrupt this illusion of authorship. For example, an arts educator in our study – like in an impromptu prayer – spontaneously uttered, “I wish there were a filter against all this kitsch” (referring to the stereotypical outputs of GAI systems). Thus inspired, we programmed several versions of “kitsch” filters that strictly transform inputs to avoid kitsch elements. Another workflow, “the opposite,” takes an input prompt and seeks the opposites of entities and their relations to one another. A third, “Overdrive,” was inspired by one participant’s observation that these workflows function similarly to musical effects units. “Overdrive” is instructed to grotesquely amplify whatever it receives as input.
Linguistic and mathematical deconstruction: These applications seek to perturb the highly normalized statistical nature of GAI systems. For example, transforming an input into “Pig Latin” proved highly disruptive for the CLIP module, yielding recognizable yet quite unexpected results. Going a step further, some workflows split the input prompt (e.g., short vs. long versions, or main vs. contextual elements) and feed these into two distinct embedding processes – first generating different mathematical representations within a model’s latent semantic space, then combining them mathematically (by a weighted linear combination). Such strategies force the system to interpolate quantitative spaces of meaning, producing highly unusual aesthetics that render aspects of the latent image space accessible and explorable within visual experience.
De-Linearization: GAI technologies are strictly designed to follow an input–output paradigm. SwarmUI/ComfyUI, e.g., as complex and modular as it is, does not allow for recursive or iterative workflows. To overcome this rigid linear-instructional logic, we instruct PromptInterception nodes to make technical–aesthetic decisions, manipulating otherwise predefined workflow parameters (from aspect ratio to prompt guidance), i.e., introducing (quasi) non-deterministic actors into an otherwise deterministic process. We also, in an exploratory manner, allow interception nodes to assess the result of the process (or any intermediate state) and to make suggestions or decisions. Using our own front end and web server – customized to respond to output signals (flags) – the system can alter the process, repeat it, or even refuse to process an input. Our intention, of course, is to break the illusory “command” chain of the user as subject and the technical apparatus as diligent object, performatively repositioning “users” and the system with respect to power within the process.
Outlook
The approaches presented are early work in progress. They are part of an ongoing empirical study that continues to unfold dynamically, especially with further participatory contributions from real-world art-education settings. To date, we have explored accessible, relatively low-threshold strategies – motivated by research and educational theory – yet largely at a proof-of-concept stage. Going beyond (merely) prompting involves further aspects that can (and should) be connected to the ideas outlined here: Regarding embedding in educational settings, the role that could or should be assigned to conventional systems – and in particular to more deconstructive systems such as ours – remains to be explored. For example, usability in educational settings entails demands beyond critical aesthetic strategies alone, including safety-related measures. Enabling exploratory attitudes with a techno-aesthetic agent that tends to intercept and alter bodily-material relations may prove difficult. With regard to bias, representation, and diversity, the limits of a prompt-centered approach pose a major challenge. Here, other ways of changing GAI systems (e.g., training or retraining language and image models, such as with LoRA technologies) may play a role – less on the purely technical side than with regard to (cultural) speaker positions: who can, and who should, determine which (mis)representations of a GAI system are to be addressed by constructive or deconstructive measures (cf. Jörissen & Baumann 2025)? The quest for greater diversity and better (or at least less harmful) GAI systems urgently demands a vastly intensified media-, culture-, and arts-educational engagement with matters of diversity and heritage. In this regard, educational GAI acts as a catalyst, putting pressure on demands that are long overdue to be addressed thoroughly.

prompt_context:
## Fokus
- **Texttyp/Ziel:** Preprint (in review); kritische Analyse und Intervention im Bereich Generative AI (GAI), speziell in Medien-, Kunst- und Kulturpädagogik.
- **Zentrale Frage/These:** Wie kann eine kritische (Programmier-)Praxis („Prompt Interception“) als kulturelle Resilienz gegen hegemoniale und ausschließende Logiken generativer KI fungieren und partizipative, nicht-extraktive Nutzungsformen in der ästhetisch-pädagogischen Praxis ermöglichen?
-, **Kernargumente:**
    1. GAI-Systeme (z.B. CLIP) sind strukturell westlich-/anglozentrisch und hegemonial verankert, was kulturelle Verzerrungen und Ausschlüsse reproduziert.
    2. Gegen rein kritische Analysen oder angepasste Nutzung existierender Systeme wird eine partizipative, gestaltende Praxis („Prompt Interception“) als notwendige Intervention positioniert.
    3. Der Fokus liegt auf der Entkopplung von Kontrolle und Output sowie der Eröffnung alternativer (Gegen-)Kontroll- und Reflexionsprozesse.
    4. Diese Praxis wird im Forschungsprojekt „AI4ArtsEd“ mittels adaptierter Open-Source-Software (ComfyUI/SwarmUI) und partizipativer Workshops operationalisiert und erprobt.
    5. Methodische Auseinandersetzung mit Bias (z.B. durch „Counterfactual Prompting“-Workshop) zeigt strukturelle Grenzen und Erlebnisse von Nicht-/Verkanntwerden auf.
    6. Wirtschaftliche und ressourcenbezogene Aspekte (Kosten, Energie, Material) stellen wesentliche Hindernisse für demokratische, inklusive Alternativen dar.

## Schlüsselbegriffe
- Generative Artificial Intelligence (GAI)
- Cultural Bias / Western Bias / Anglo-centrism
- CLIP (Contrastive Language–Image Pre-training)
- Prompt Interception
- Counterfactual Prompting
- Media and Arts Education (Kultur- und Medienpädagogik)
- Participatory Design / Non-extractivist AI
- Cultural Resilience
- Representation / Misrecognition / Diversity
- Decolonial Approaches / Indigenous AI
- LAION Dataset / Training Corpora
- Stable Diffusion / Text-to-Image Generation
- Open Source AI / ComfyUI / SwarmUI
- Relational Approach / Educational Situations

## Vorhandene Referenzen und Debatten
- **Autor:innen & Werke:** Radford et al. (2021, CLIP); Ghosh (2024, Caste & AI); Abdilla et al. (2020, Indigenous AI); Crawford (2021, Atlas of AI); Morozov (2013, Solutionism); Galloway & Thacker (2007, Networks).
- **Schulen/Begriffe:** Kritische KI-Forschung; Dekoloniale KI-Perspektiven; Postdigitale Pädagogik; Relationale/Theoretische Medienpädagogik (Jörissen & Meyer, 2015).
- **Genannte Projekte:** „AI4ArtsEd“ (BMBF-gefördert); „Time Machine Europe“; Indigenous AI-Initiativen.

## Empirischer/methodischer Kontext
- **Material/Feld:** Generative Bild-KI (Stable Diffusion 1.5), CLIP-Technologie, LAION-Datensatz.
- **Methoden:** Partizipative, bottom-up Forschungsdesigns; transdisziplinäre Workshops (z.B. in Nairobi 2024); iterative Softwareentwicklung; „Counterfactual Prompting“ als Analysemethode; serielle Bildanalyse; qualitativ-interpretative Auswertung von Gruppendiskussionen.
- **Fallbeispiele:** Workshop „Generative AI as Cultural and Educational Agent“ mit systematisch generierten Bildserien (Prompt-Varianten wie „a person at work“ mit nationalen/soziokulturellen Zusätzen); Entwicklung und Erprobung von „Prompt Interception“-Nodes in ComfyUI.

## Offene Anschlussstellen
- Grenzen prompt-zentrierter Ansätze für Repräsentation/Diversität; Bedarf nach alternativen Modell-Interventionen (z.B. LoRA, Training/Retraining).
- Einbettung und Nutzbarkeit dekonstruktiver Systeme in konkreten Bildungskontexten (u.a. Sicherheit, Exploration).
- Kulturelle Sprecherpositionen: Wer bestimmt, welche (Fehl-)Repräsentationen adressiert werden?
- Verstärkter Bedarf an medien-/kulturpädagogischer Auseinandersetzung mit Diversität und Heritage, katalysiert durch GAI.
- Wirtschaftliche und ressourcenbezogene Herausforderungen für gemeinwohlorientierte, inklusive KI-Entwicklung.

## Argumenteinheiten
-_ Die strukturelle Verankerung kultureller Hegemonie in GAI: Analyse von CLIP und dessen Trainingsdaten (LAION) zeigt systematische westlich-/anglozentrische und ausschließende Bias als Designprinzip.
-_ Die Notwendigkeit gestaltender Intervention über reine Kritik hinaus: Forderung nach partizipativer, nicht-extraktiver Auseinandersetzung und eigener Softwareentwicklung (Open Source) im pädagogischen Kontext.
-_ Empirische Demonstration von Bias und

_ Die Notwendigkeit gestaltender Intervention über reine Kritik hinaus: Forderung nach partizipativer, nicht-extraktiver Auseinandersetzung und eigener Softwareentwicklung (Open Source) im pädagogischen Kontext.
